ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lamb chops masala. FOODON_03302051 (lamb dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents cooked sheep/lamb meat as the closest parent; no specific masala preparation code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for lamb chops masala as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of lamb chops masala consists of 2 to 3 bone-in lamb chops weighing approximately 300 g, served with sliced tomatoes, onion rings, and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton (Goat/Lamb), cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked mutton/lamb are used as the primary nutrient basis. Values are adapted to account for the masala marinade (spices, tomato, onion, yoghurt) which contributes minor additional carbohydrate, sodium, and fat. Spice contribution to macros is minor and within estimation tolerance. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 17174, Lamb, domestic, loin chops, cooked, broiled) was used as a cross-reference for protein and fat ranges.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Protein-Rich Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for cooked lamb with spices
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb chops masala. Lamb and other lean meats are consistently classified as low-GI foods (GI < 20) in the University of Sydney GI Database due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content. A GI of 15 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate for cooked lamb with masala spices, consistent with published GI values for cooked meat dishes. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.4) / 100 ≈ 1.3, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 2.8 × 3 = 8.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from masala spices and marinade
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of lamb chops masala provides approximately 960 mg sodium, which represents 42–48% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb chops masala has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 15) and a glycemic load of approximately 1.3 per serving, making it a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the fat content should be monitored within overall dietary targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from lamb
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 15.6 g saturated fat and 255 mg cholesterol, which may approach or exceed daily limits for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing leaner cuts, trimming visible fat, and limiting portion size are recommended strategies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Chops Masala (Spicy) is a North Indian restaurant dish consisting of bone-in lamb chops that are marinated in a spiced yoghurt-based masala blend — typically incorporating ginger, garlic, chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and turmeric — and then broiled or grilled at high heat to produce charred, flavourful edges while retaining juicy interior meat. The dish is served with sliced tomatoes, onion rings, and fresh herbs such as coriander. The flavour profile is bold, smoky, and intensely spiced, with a characteristic heat from chilli and depth from the dry-roasted spice blend. The texture of the lamb is tender and slightly charred on the exterior. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at North Indian restaurants and curry houses, particularly along South Bridge Road, and is priced in the premium segment (SGD 31–36 per serving). Nutritionally, lamb chops masala is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The dish provides approximately 25.5 g protein and 12.5 g fat per 100 g. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 15), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the spice marinade, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The saturated fat content from lamb warrants attention for those with cardiovascular disease. The dish is typically halal and gluten-free, making it accessible to Muslim diners and those with gluten intolerance.

confidence_reason: No exact match for lamb chops masala was found in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mutton/lamb data cross-referenced with USDA broiled lamb loin chop values (NDB 17174), with minor adjustments for masala marinade contribution. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database classification of cooked meat as low-GI. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA data for cooked lamb preparations and reliable values are not available from approved sources. All other -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated or cross-referenced numeric values from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant portions for 2–3 bone-in lamb chops; actual restaurant portions may vary. The masala spice blend contributes minor additional carbohydrate (from onion, tomato) and sodium (from salt in marinade); these contributions are incorporated into the adapted per-100g values. The dish is classified as minimally processed as it involves fresh lamb marinated and broiled without industrial processing. Halal status is inferred from the North Indian Muslim culinary tradition of the serving restaurants; individual restaurant certification should be verified. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (15 × 8.4) / 100 = 1.3, where 8.4 g carbohydrate = 2.8 g/100g × 300 g serving.
